---
title: "Your Research Title Here"
subtitle: "A Concise Subtitle Describing the Contribution"
author:
  - name: "First Author"
    affiliation: "University / Institute"
  - name: "Second Author"
    affiliation: "University / Institute"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: ../themes/impeccable-academic.scss
    slide-number: c/t
    hash: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    center: false
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: default
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: github-dark
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
        <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Cormorant+Garamond:ital,wght@0,300..700;1,300..700&family=Source+Sans+3:ital,wght@0,200..900;1,200..900&family=JetBrains+Mono:ital,wght@0,100..800;1,100..800&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
bibliography: references.bib
---

## Motivation & Background {.section-header}

::: {.notes}
Start with the problem space. Why should the audience care?
Establish the gap in existing knowledge or the practical need.
:::

---

## Research Question

:::: {.two-col}
::: {.div}
### The Problem

Current approaches to **[problem domain]** face significant limitations:

- Limitation one: scalability under real-world conditions
- Limitation two: lack of generalizability across populations
- Limitation three: computational overhead in practical deployment

[This is a known challenge in the field.]{.subtle}
:::

::: {.keybox}
**Research Question**

*How can we [achieve specific goal] while maintaining [key constraint] across [target context]?*

This question addresses the gap between theoretical advances and practical deployment.
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Frame the research question clearly. The audience should understand exactly
what you set out to answer and why it matters.
:::

---

## Literature Context

:::: {.two-col}
::: {.div}
### Prior Work

- **Smith et al. (2023)** — First to demonstrate [X] in [context]
- **Lee & Kim (2024)** — Extended the approach to [Y], but limited by [Z]
- **Johnson (2024)** — Proposed [method], achieving state-of-the-art on [benchmark]

::: {.citation}
See @smith2023 for a comprehensive review of the field.
:::
:::

::: {.div}
### Our Contribution

::: {.tipbox}
**Novel Contributions**

1. We introduce [method name], a new approach to [problem]
2. We provide theoretical guarantees for [property]
3. We validate on [N] real-world datasets across [K] domains
:::
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Briefly situate your work within the literature. Highlight what prior
work achieved and where gaps remain. Transition to your contributions.
:::

---

## Methodology {.section-header}

::: {.notes}
Walk through the methodology step by step. Keep each step
to one or two sentences on the slide — detail goes in speaker notes.
:::

---

## Proposed Framework

::: {.methodbox}
**Method Overview**

Our approach consists of three stages:

1. **Data preprocessing** — Normalize and partition input data using [technique]
2. **Model architecture** — [Architecture type] with [key modification]
3. **Training procedure** — [Optimization method] with [regularization strategy]
:::

[Formal definitions follow on the next slide.]{.subtle}

::: {.notes}
Give a high-level overview first, then dive into each component.
The audience needs the big picture before the details.
:::

---

## Formal Definition

### Stage 1: Representation Learning

Given input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}$, we learn a mapping $f_\theta: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ such that:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p(\mathbf{x})} \left[ \ell(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}), y) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta) \right]
$$

where $\Omega(\theta)$ is the regularization term controlling model complexity.

::: {.warningbox}
**Assumption**

We assume the data distribution is stationary during training. Non-stationary settings require the extension described in Appendix B.
:::

::: {.notes}
Walk through the formal definition step by step. Explain each term
in plain language before moving to the next equation.
:::

---

## Experimental Setup

| Component | Configuration |
|-----------|--------------|
| Dataset | [Name], N = 50,000 samples |
| Architecture | [Type] with [layers/params] |
| Optimizer | AdamW, lr = 3e-4, weight decay = 0.01 |
| Training | 100 epochs, batch size 256 |
| Hardware | 4× NVIDIA A100 (80GB) |
| Evaluation | 5-fold cross-validation |

::: {.footnote}
Code and data available at: [repository-url]
:::

::: {.notes}
Briefly summarize the experimental setup. Emphasize any non-standard
choices and why they were made. Mention reproducibility details.
:::

---

## Results {.section-header}

::: {.notes}
Transition: "Now let's look at what the experiments tell us."
:::

---

## Main Results

:::: {.two-col}
::: {.div}
### Quantitative Performance

| Method | Accuracy | F1 | AUC |
|--------|----------|-----|-----|
| Baseline A | 82.3 | 0.81 | 0.89 |
| Baseline B | 85.1 | 0.84 | 0.91 |
| **Ours** | **89.7** | **0.89** | **0.95** |

[All metrics averaged over 5 runs ± std.]{.smaller}
:::

::: {.keybox}
**Key Finding**

Our method achieves a [**+4.6%**]{.hi} improvement in accuracy over the strongest baseline, with consistent gains across all metrics.

The improvement is statistically significant (p < 0.001, paired t-test).
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Lead with the headline number. Then provide context for why the
improvement is meaningful in this domain.
:::

---

## Comparison Analysis

:::: {.comparison}
::: {.compare-left}
### Previous SOTA

- Accuracy: 85.1%
- Requires 3× more compute
- Limited to single-domain data
- No theoretical guarantees
:::

::: {.compare-right}
### Our Approach

- Accuracy: [**89.7%**]{.hi-green}
- 2× faster training time
- Cross-domain generalization
- Convergence guarantees (Theorem 1)
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
Use this slide to directly contrast your approach with prior SOTA.
Highlight not just accuracy but practical advantages.
:::

---

## Discussion & Limitations

::: {.tipbox}
**Strengths**

- Consistent improvement across all evaluation metrics and domains
- Theoretical grounding provides interpretability guarantees
- Practical deployment requires modest computational resources
:::

::: {.warningbox}
**Limitations**

- Performance on [edge case] degrades when [condition]
- Requires [minimum data size] for reliable convergence
- Not yet tested in [specific real-world scenario]
:::

::: {.notes}
Be honest about limitations — it builds credibility.
Mention future work directions that address each limitation.
:::

---

## Conclusion

### Summary of Contributions

1. **[Method Name]** — a novel approach achieving state-of-the-art performance
2. **Theoretical analysis** — convergence and generalization bounds
3. **Comprehensive evaluation** — validated across [N] datasets and [K] domains

::: {.keybox}
**Takeaway**

[One sentence that captures the essential insight of this work — what should the audience remember a week from now?]
:::

::: {.notes}
End with the one thing you want the audience to remember.
If they forget everything else, what should stick?
:::

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {.references}
Include your BibTeX references in `references.bib` alongside this file.
Quarto will automatically format citations used with `@key` syntax.
:::

[Thank you — Questions?]{.larger}

::: {.notes}
Acknowledge collaborators and funding sources.
Be ready for questions on methodology and limitations.
:::
